planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; autumn fern is one of the most adaptable shade ferns, tolerating summer heat, humidity, and periodic drought better than most species.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; autumn fern is outstanding for its spectacular copper-red to orange new fronds that emerge in spring and again in late summer — the intense metallic color fades to lustrous, glossy dark green as fronds mature, giving the plant two distinct seasons of visual interest. It is one of the most heat-tolerant and adaptable shade ferns, performing well across much of the eastern U.S. including the Gulf South where many ferns struggle. Evergreen to semi-evergreen in mild winters; the old fronds can be cut to the ground in late winter to make way for the spectacular spring flush of colored new growth. Tolerates dry shade better than most ferns once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; sensitive fern naturalizes aggressively in wet areas by underground rhizomes and can become invasive in bog gardens.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun near ponds, streams, and in rain garden edges; sensitive fern produces large, broadly triangular, bright-green fronds that are coarser and less typical-looking than most ferns and die back at the first frost (hence the name 'sensitive'). Separate fertile fronds bear persistent clusters of small, bead-like brown spore cases that remain standing through winter, providing attractive winter silhouette and food structure for birds. The plant spreads aggressively by underground rhizomes in consistently moist conditions and is best used in large-scale naturalistic plantings where its spread can be accommodated. The hardiest fern for wet, disturbed sites across North America.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; Virginia chain fern thrives in bogs, wet woodland depressions, and the permanently moist margins of streams and ponds.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Virginia chain fern is a native North American fern of bogs, swamps, and wet woodland floors, producing distinctive fronds with alternating sterile and fertile pinnae that create a chain-like pattern — the source of its common name. The fertile fronds are somewhat narrower and browner than the sterile ones and remain erect through winter, providing structure and winter interest. Spreads by rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing colonies in appropriate wet, shaded conditions. Excellent for naturalizing wet, shaded areas of the garden where few other plants thrive. Tolerates standing water seasonally. The larger related species W. fimbriata (giant chain fern) is native to the Pacific Coast.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; New York fern naturalizes quickly by creeping rhizomes to form extensive colonies in shaded woodland conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; New York fern is an Eastern North American native that forms extensive, light-green colonies by creeping rhizomes in woodland soils, creating a soft, airy groundcover that fills large areas quickly. The fronds are distinctive for tapering to a point at both the tip and base (unlike most ferns, which are widest at the middle or base) — a characteristic sometimes called the 'New Yorker' pattern. The plant goes fully dormant in winter. It tolerates both slightly dryer and slightly wetter conditions than many ferns, making it versatile for shaded borders, woodland gardens, and naturalistic plantings under deciduous trees. Ideal for mass planting where a uniform, low-maintenance woodland floor effect is desired.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; male fern is one of the most adaptable of all ferns, tolerating dry shade, poor soils, and challenging urban conditions that defeat most other species.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; male fern is one of the most widely grown and adaptable ferns in the world, forming large, vase-shaped, semi-evergreen clumps with lance-shaped, dark-green fronds that are both elegant and extremely tough. It tolerates dry shade, poor soils, periods of neglect, and more urban pollution than virtually any other fern. New fronds emerge as tightly coiled croziers (fiddleheads) in spring. Semi-evergreen in mild winters; cut old fronds to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges to improve appearance. Excellent for difficult, challenging shaded spots. Named varieties include 'Barnesii' (narrow, upright) and 'Linearis Polydactyla' (narrow, crested frond tips). Divide clumps every 5–7 years in spring.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
